duties: Two ~40-60 hour TA positions are available for ECE311H1 - Introduction to Linear Control Systems Course Description: An introduction to dynamic systems and their control. Properties of feedback control systems. The concept of stability of feedback systems, Routh-Hurwitz stability criterion. Internal model principle and steady state tracking. Frequency response and stability in the frequency domain. Feedback control design in the frequency domain using Bode and Nyquist plots. Labs include control design using MATLAB/Simulink and Arduino. Job Requirements and Job Description: The TA positions are open to revise, update and modify existent labs, and to develop a new experimental lab for ECE311. The purpose of the new lab is to design and implement a controller and perform experimental evaluation of its performance. As experiment, one of the existing mechanical experiments will be used (e.g. cart-on-wheels/pendulum). The lab will be implemented on an existing micro-controller board (Arduino). TA duties are as follows: • Revise existent labs, Matlab/Simulink and Arduino code, and fix any bugs. • Redesign/update existing labs and an introductory lab. • Revise/update existent lab documents, files, Matlab/Simulink code and Arduino code. • Develop new lab, control design/implementation in Matlab/Simulink. • Implement new lab on Arduino (interface with Matlab/Simulink) - using an existing mechanical experiment (cart-on-wheels/pendulum). • Develop new lab setup, prepare all materials, lab files, documents, code. Please send a short resume specifying your qualifications and related experience to Professor Lacra Pavel at pavel@ece.utoronto.ca and Professor Luca Scardovi at luca.scardovi@utoronto.ca
experience: Previous experience is the more relevant criterion than the need to acquire experience in respect of this posted position.
qualifications_minimum: Familiarity with Arduino micro-controller board, low-level, C-programming, embedded control techniques. Previous control experience (either as TA or RA). Matlab/Simulink proficiency. Experience in developing/maintaining laboratories for control courses (e.g. ECE311/ECE356/ECE410/ECE557).
salery: $53.92 per hour + vacation pay. Should rates stipulated in the Collective Agreement vary from rates stated in this posting, the rates stated in the Collective Agreement shall prevail.

job_title: Introduction to Critical Issues in Caribbean Health
application_procedure:
Applications must include: 
•	a cover letter that provides: 
•	a detailed description of their proposed course with a list of potential topics and modes of assessment, 
•	an explanation of the course’s relevance to the hosting undergraduate program (Caribbean Studies), and 
•	course learning outcomes  
•	a complete and current curriculum vitae 
•	transcript(s) 
•	a letter of reference from one (1) referee; and 
•	any other relevant materials the candidate wishes to submit for consideration such as a statement of teaching philosophy and student evaluations 
 
All applications must be submitted through the New College Teaching Fellowship Application form: https://forms.cloud.microsoft/r/cuhizZm3rk
duties:
In partnership with the Centre for Caribbean Studies, the New College Teaching Fellowship invites applications from suitably qualified PhD candidates at the University of Toronto who are interested in teaching an in-person first-year undergraduate seminar in the intersection of Health and Caribbean Studies for the 2027 and 2028 Winter Terms. As part of the application, fellows will be able to propose a course theme of their own. 

New College Teaching Fellows will be the instructors of record, carry full responsibility for planning, preparing class materials, teaching, meeting with and/or responding to student queries, grading assignments, and reporting grades for the following class: 

CAR130H1 - Introduction to Critical Issues in Caribbean Health. 

Exploring a new theme each year, this course looks at the intersection of STEM fields and Caribbean Studies, showing how thinking critically about the historical, social, political, epistemic or economic stakes involved in Caribbean Health, brings fresh insights to the design, analysis and social impact of health policy. Themes include: the social and political determinants of health, post/pandemic health policy making, access to medicine and medical technologies, digital technologies, climate change, infectious and or chronic diseases, reproductive health, mental health, ageing and disabilities.

New College Teaching Fellows are the instructors of record for the course and carry full responsibility for planning, preparing class materials, teaching, meeting with and/or responding to student queries, grading assignments, and reporting grades. Teaching Fellows are expected to comply with all University of Toronto Policies. They will also be participating in New Pedagogy, which is New College’s community of practice exploring teaching and learning at the College and beyond, as well as participate in events, lectures, and initiatives sponsored by the College as well as the Centre for Caribbean Studies.

It is understood that some announcements of vacancies are tentative, pending final course determinations and enrolment.
experience: Past teaching experience is the more relevant criterion than the need to acquire experience in respect of this posted position.
qualifications_minimum:
We are interested in applicants who ask fundamental questions about the historical, social, political, epistemic or economic stakes involved in Caribbean Health, in ways that are oriented toward realising greater social justice whether in health policy, or in broader social or political domains. Potential focal areas of interest might include but are not limited to domains such as coloniality, environmental justice, reproductive justice, disability rights, LGBTQ rights, anti-racism, decolonisation, migration. The range of health topics that might be explored is also open, and could include such topics as social and political determinants of health, post/pandemic health policy-making, health care infrastructures and supply chains, access to medicines and medical technologies, digital technologies, climate change, or other topics, in domains including but not limited to infectious disease, chronic disease, reproductive health, mental health, ageing and the life course. An ability to illustrate the importance of STEM literacy to social justice outcomes in Caribbean Health will be considered an asset. For more information about the unit, visit the homepage for the Centre for Caribbean Studies.  

As such the minimum qualifications for the role consist of:
•	The candidate must be eligible for and apply to the New College Teaching Fellowship 
•	The candidate must have achieved PhD candidacy before the start of the Teaching Fellowship in a relevant discipline or interdisciplinary field with particular expertise in areas listed above.
qualifications_preferred:
•	Demonstrated interpersonal and teaching skills, including the ability to collaborate and work with faculty members, staff, and students with diverse backgrounds.
•	Teaching experience at the university setting with awareness of needs of first-year students.
salery: $10,553.15 + vacation pay for the first appointment (inclusive of first appointment stipend) and $9,442.98 for the second appointment.
tutorial: Lectures: Wednesdays 3:00 PM - 5:00 PM [Note: the course delivery mode is currently in person]. The lecture timing for the Winter 2028 offering is to be determined, but the successful applicant would need to be available to teach January 1, 2028 to April 30, 2028.

job_title: Re-Presenting Ourselves as Treaty People in Tkaron:to
application_procedure: Please submit a cover letter, CV, and the contact information for two references to typ.office@utoronto.ca by Thursday, April 30, 2026
duties:
The Teaching Assistant will support the course instructor in the instruction and management of TYP017H1 (Re-Presenting Ourselves as Treaty People in Tkaron:to), a compulsory course at the Transitional Year Program at the University of Toronto: St. George. This course locates students within the fraught history of Indigenous-Settler encounters on Indigenous homelands situating them as Treaty people (with inherited responsibilities to the lands upon which they live and work and to the original peoples who continue their stewardship of those lands). Why is the land acknowledgement (whether it is communicated by Indigenous individuals or by many settler-run institutions) so important—here in Tkaran:to and across Turtle Island (North America)? How do the creations stories of the Anishinaabeg, Haudenosaunee, and Wendat peoples inform our understanding of living ethically in the world, of thinking the highest thought, and of successfully pursuing minobimaatisiiwin (the way of good life)? Resources, course delivery (pedagogy), modes of evaluation, and learning objectives are based in Indigenous systems of thought. Structured upon a land-based pedagogical model this course provides an experiential introduction to Indigenous thought, cosmology, lifeway, knowledge systems and governance/guardianship systems and provides students with the tools to process this knowledge and to articulate it for themselves and for others. Learners continue their journey through the history of contact, colonization, and finally into an understanding of what it means to be a treaty person living in Canada today.
 
 The Teaching Assistant (TA) will support the course instructor in the general instruction and management of the course. The TA will help to lead classes that support TYP students in their development of academic and life skills, including notetaking, the writing process, research, organization, and other related skills. The TA will evaluate student work, provide feedback, schedule and hold office hours, provide academic support for course assignments, and lead discussions, and manage the course Quercus. Additional duties will be determined in consultation with the course instructor and registrar. The TA will be required to attend lectures, and occasionally staff meetings. Please note, this course will be offered in person, with online elements (i.e. Quercus). For more information about TYP, please visit typ.utoronto.ca.

job_title: Educate to Liberate: Hip-Hop, Hip-Hop Culture, and the Black Radical Tradition
application_procedure: Please submit a cover letter, CV, and the contact information for two references in the same PDF to typ.office@utoronto.ca by Thursday, April 30, 2026.
duties:
TYPXXXH1S (Educate to Liberate: Hip-Hop, Hip-Hop Culture, and the Black Radical Tradition) is an elective course for TYP students that explores key concepts, ideas, and methods within Black Studies  and critical Hip-Hop education. The course examines Hip-Hop’s roots in the Black radical tradition, African diasporic cultural production, and its connections to community, identity, resistance, and liberation. Students will develop a critical understanding of Hip-Hop as a site of knowledge production by engaging with its social, political, and historical contexts. Through analysis of music, lyrics, visual culture, scholarly texts and community organizing, the course strengthens skills in critical reading, writing, and critical analysis. Grounded in culturally responsive and anti-colonial approaches, the course encourages reflections on power, inequity, and liberatory social transformation. The course instructor will develop a course syllabus to instruct and support student learning while fostering an encouraging, creative, and culturally responsive environment that reflects Hip-Hop pedagogies and diverse learning needs.
Prepare, facilitate, and mark activities and assignments that support student success in academic writing, critical analysis, and creative expression within Black Studies, cultural studies, and social sciences. Support students in engaging with Hip-Hop as a site of knowledge production, including analysis of music, lyrics, visual culture, and performance within their social and political contexts. Counsel students with respect to academic progress, including study skills, critical reading, organization, and time management, and connecting them to appropriate supports for non-academic matters where needed. Evaluate attendance and course participation, with attention to inclusive and community-based learning practices. Attend scheduled faculty and staff meetings and contributing to collaborative course planning.
Submitting marks for assessments associated with the course. Other duties will be determined in consultation with TYP's Director and Registrar. Please note, this course will be offered in person, with online administrative elements (i.e. Quercus). For more information about TYP, please visit typ.utoronto.ca.

Familiarity with Quercus and online instructional methods is also required.
qualifications_preferred: The candidate must have: (a) Previous work experience and/or coursework relating to Black Studies, Hip-Hop Education, or related disciplines. duties:
TYP005H1F (Quantitative Reasoning) is an elective course for Transitional Year Programme (TYP) students that develops foundational skills in numeracy, mathematical thinking, and data literacy. The course uses a class “textbook” on Quercus, along with handouts and web-based resources, to support learning. Students may also engage with AI tools (e.g., ChatGPT, Gemini, Llama, DeepSeek, or similar platforms) to enhance their understanding and support their learning process.
The course introduces students to the contributions of diverse civilizations to the development of mathematics and fosters an appreciation of its historical growth. It aims to strengthen core mathematical knowledge in areas such as arithmetic, elementary algebra, geometry, and trigonometry, sets, and functions. Students will build problem-solving skills and apply them in real-world contexts, as well as learn to collect, present, and interpret data. The course also encourages critical reflection on the role and impact of technology in learning quantitative skills.
TYP016H1S (Advanced Quantitative Reasoning) builds on the foundations established in TYP005H1F and further develops students’ analytical, mathematical, and data literacy skills. Students engage with more advanced quantitative concepts, including deeper work in functions, data analysis, and applied problem-solving, with continued emphasis on real-world applications and critical engagement with quantitative information. The course instructor will develop a course syllabus to instruct and support student learning while fostering an encouraging, inclusive, and accessible environment that reflects diverse learning needs in quantitative education. Prepare, facilitate and mark activities and assignments that support student success in quantitative reasoning, problem-solving, data interpretation, and analytical thinking. Support students in engaging with quantitative concepts and real-world data, including interpreting graphs, statistics, and numerical arguments across disciplines. Counsel students with respect to academic progress, including study skills, problem-solving strategies, organization, and time management, and connecting them to appropriate supports for non-academic matters where needed. Evaluate attendance and course participation, with attention to inclusive and active learning practices. Attend scheduled faculty and staff meetings and contributing to collaborative course planning. Other duties will be determined in consultation with TYP's Director and Registrar. Please note, this course will be offered in person, with online administrative elements (i.e. Quercus). For more information about TYP, please visit typ.utoronto.ca.

application_procedure: 1. See TA job postings on the department website at https://www.economics.utoronto.ca/index.php/index/graduate/taOpenings and CUPE3902Unit1 postings at http://unit1.hrandequity.utoronto.ca/. 2. Regular TA Postings - All applicants registered in our Economics graduate programs (MA, MFE, PHD) must submit information via an electronic link sent to their UofT email address by the TA Coordinator.  3. Regular TA Postings - Non-Economics graduate and undergraduate student applicants must submit an application to the Economics TA Coordinator using the application link on https://www.economics.utoronto.ca/index.php/index/graduate/taOpenings, including specifying which TA position(s) you are applying for; your UtorID, providing your U of T (only) email address; and your current program of study.  4. NOTE: For all Emergency TA Postings all applicants submit application information to the Economics TA Coordinator (the URL link for the application form is provided in the emergency job posting), specifying which TA position(s) you are applying for; your UtorID, providing your U of T (only) email address; and your current program of study. 5. UG course descriptions can be found at https://artsci.calendar.utoronto.ca/section/Economics and https://www.economics.utoronto.ca/index.php/index/undergraduate/courses/1
duties: Duties will be arranged by the instructor and may include grading tests, exams, and papers, holding office hours, holding tutorials, invigilating tests and exams, and other duties related to the course. Duties may have an online component. Completion of all grading of all student work that is completed during the term of the appointment, up to and including final exams is expected.
experience: Previous experience is the more relevant criterion than the need to acquire experience in respect of this posted position.
qualifications_minimum: Academic qualifications, experience in invigilation, grading and tutoring in a similar course
qualifications_preferred: Graduate-level knowledge of Economics
salery: $53.92/hr (+4% vacation pay). Please note that should rates stipulated in the collective agreement vary from rates stated in this posting, the rates stated in the collective agreement shall prevail.

salery: SGS II rate ($53.92) + vacation pay. Should rates stipulated in the Collective Agreement vary from rates stated in this posting, the rates stated in the Collective Agreement shall prevail.

application_procedure: Applicants should submit a cover letter and curriculum vitae via email to: Jenn English (jenn.english@utoronto.ca), Payroll Officer, Department of Ecology and Evolutionary Biology, University of Toronto, 25 Willcocks St., Toronto, Ontario, Canada M5S 3B2
duties: In advance of course, participate in the design of group teaching with tenure-stream faculty members, who are also course Team Leaders. During the course, reside at field station; deliver impromptu and organized lectures on aspects of temperate ecology and evolution; develop customized course content related to their prior expertise under dynamic field conditions; develop and deliver organized and impromptu labs on the use of R, including data visualization and analysis; unload equipment and set up teaching activities on site; pack and load equipment at end of course. Preparation and delivery of field activities; help advise, assist and supervise activities in the field including student research projects; available for office hours; assist with marking and commenting on group and individual oral presentations as appropriate and in accordance with University regulations as described in the Faculty of Arts & Science Academic Handbook.
experience: Past teaching experience is the more relevant criterion than the need to acquire experience in respect of this posted position.
qualifications_minimum: A strong academic background in ecology and evolutionary biology is required, specifically a strong background in field research in ecology and evolutionary biology. Strong computational and/or statistical skills, as well as background in teaching R to undergraduates. A good knowledge of the field ecology of at least some groups of terrestrial organisms (e.g., plants, insects, birds) or aquatic organisms (including amphibians) that are easily studied at KSR.
qualifications_preferred: Research experience (experimental or monitoring) with organisms present at the Koffler Scientific Reserve and that occur in summer is an asset. Prior experience teaching this or a similar university-level field course is preferred.
tutorial: This course will take place at the Koffler Scientific Reserve located in King City, Ontario, Canada for the duration of August 1-14, 2026; pre-course meetings (including 1-2 on site days at KSR) are expected to begin in late May 2026. Transportation, meals and accommodations will be provided by the department for the duration of the course.

application_procedure: Applications are to be completed online https://www.utsc.utoronto.ca/webapps/cupehiring/dept/cms/app/instr
duties: All normal duties related to the design and teaching of an in-person University credit course, including: Preparation and delivery of course content (in-person), holding regular office hours, responding to student inquiries, and course administration such as developing an appropriate grading scheme, handling accommodations, writing and administering exams including any deferred tests related to this particular offering, and managing/supervising TAs assigned to the course. Coordination with full-time faculty to ensure the course content aligns with upstream expectations is required.
experience: Past teaching experience is the more relevant criterion than the need to acquire experience in respect of this posted position.
qualifications_minimum: The successful candidate must demonstrate thorough, solid, and technical expertise in the topics explored during the course. These include principles of digital circuits and their operation (transistor-based models for simple logic gates), binary system, binary encoding of standard data types; Boolean Logic and how it is used to build circuits that perform computation (boolean functions, logical and arithmetic operations). Memory systems and their implementation using latches and flip-flops; memory addressing and memory banks. CPU organization and operation including data-path, instruction execution cycle, and advanced CPU architecture topics including pipelining and caching; and assembly language programming. The successful candidate must demonstrate thorough familiarity with the course material. Previous experience teaching a similar course, or relevant research experience at the post-graduate level is a strong asset. The applicant must be a graduate student or PDF in a field related to the material covered in the course

job_title: Design and Analysis of Data Structures
application_procedure: Applications are to be completed online https://www.utsc.utoronto.ca/webapps/cupehiring/dept/cms/app/instr
duties: All normal duties related to the design and teaching of an in-person University credit course, including: Preparation and delivery of course content (in-person), holding regular office hours, responding to student inquiries, and course administration such as developing an appropriate grading scheme, handling accommodations, writing and administering exams including any deferred tests related to this particular offering, and managing/supervising TAs assigned to the course. Coordination with full-time faculty to ensure the course content aligns with upstream expectations is required.
experience: Past teaching experience is the more relevant criterion than the need to acquire experience in respect of this posted position.
qualifications_minimum:
The successful candidate must demonstrate thorough, solid, and technical expertise in the topics explored during the course. These include the design, analysis, and comparison of efficient data structures. Complexity analysis, including Big O and Big theta notation and their corresponding use in algorithm analysis; detailed study of balanced trees, hash tables, heaps and priority queues; graphs (including common graph based algorithms such as spanning trees, path-finding, and shortest path computation), and amortized complexity. The successful candidate must demonstrate thorough familiarity with the course material. Previous experience teaching a similar course, or relevant research experience at the post-graduate level is a strong asset.
An undergraduate degree in Computer Science or a related field is required. First consideration will be given to applicants who have graduate-level expertise in a field related to the material covered in the course.

duties:
CSE345H1F – Equity and Activism in Education

Examines contemporary issues in education and schooling from a social justice and equity perspective. Engages with a variety of theoretical frameworks including anti-homophobia education, critical pedagogy, critical race theory, decolonizing knowledges, and intersectionality. Includes an overview of educational activist projects. 

DUTIES: Normal duties related to the design and teaching of a university credit course, including preparation and delivery of course content; development, administration and marking of assignments, tests and exams; calculation and submission of grades; and holding regular office hours.
experience: Past teaching experience is the more relevant criterion than the need to acquire experience in respect of this posted position.
qualifications_minimum: Completed or nearly completed Ph.D. (or equivalent) required. Academic background in social justice education and familiarity with anti-oppressive pedagogies. The candidate should have a research and/or teaching background in or across the following areas of scholarship: critical anti-racist education; decolonial pedagogies; gender in education; anti-homophobia education; critical disability studies in education; pedagogies of solidarity.
qualifications_preferred:
Experience teaching social justice issues in a university setting is preferred.
 
This course is being offered to both students enrolled in the Faculty of Arts and Science as well as students currently enrolled in the Transitional Year Programme (TYP). TYP is a full-time, eight-month access-to-University program intended for adults who do not have the formal qualifications for university admission. The program has its roots in the Black community in Toronto, having emerged out of two community summer programs in 1969 and 1970, which prepared a small number of students for entry into York University. TYP is meant for those who were unable to acquire the opportunity to finish high school and or gain the necessary qualifications to enter university due to financial problems, family difficulties or other circumstances resulting from systemic inequity. The Program actively encourages applications from members of the Indigenous, Black and LGBTQ communities, sole-support parents, persons with disabilities, neurodiversity, and individuals from working-class and under-class backgrounds of all ethnicities.

job_title: An Introduction to Statistical Reasoning and Data Science
application_procedure:
Application information is available at https://www.statistics.utoronto.ca/employment-opportunities/cupe-positions-unit-1. 
The deadline to submit your application is September 26, 2022. For more information, you may contact: Angela Niazmand, Undergraduate and TA Administrator, Department of Statistical Sciences, University of Toronto
700 University Ave- Rm 9160- 6, Ontario Power Building, Toronto, ON M5G 1Z5, www.statistics.utoronto.ca, E-mail: jobapps.statistics@utoronto.ca

If during the application and/or selection process you require accommodation due to a disability, please contact
jobapps.statistics@utoronto.ca
This job is posted in accordance with the CUPE 3
duties:
Duties are in person unless otherwise indicated. Duties will include any or all of the following: attending lectures, running 
tutorial sessions, facilitating and evaluating student engagement on on-line discussion boards, participating in training 
sessions, meeting with the instructors, holding office hours, grading student homework including written reports, and 
assisting with grading of tests and exams.
experience: The need to acquire experience is the more relevant criterion than previous experience in respect of this posted position.
qualifications_minimum: Senior undergraduate students (third and fourth year) with strong background in statistics and exemplary communication skills may also be considered.
qualifications_preferred: Graduate student with strong background in statistics, as well as excellent communication and writing skills, and the ability to interact well with undergraduate students.
